STAT380_MP2

Isaac Swope

Andrew Loiselle

2026-03-26

Front Matter

# Libraries
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(ggplot2)
library(readxl)
library(lubridate)
## 
## Attaching package: 'lubridate'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     date, intersect, setdiff, union
library(knitr)
library(patchwork)
library(mdsr)
## Warning: package 'mdsr' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(tidyr)

# Functions
countNA <- function(col){
  return(sum(is.na(col)))
}

# Data
games_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Swopei/STAT380-MP2_repo/main/Data/CODGames2_mp.xlsx"
games_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx")
download.file(games_url, destfile = games_tmp, mode = "wb")
Games <- read_excel(games_tmp)

Task 1

The reason that we should not use the na.omit function for this dataset is because it will unnecessarily create bias in the dataset and remove rows with valuable data, greatly reducing the sample size. We would not want to remove an entire row because only one variable has missing data, which would lead to a large number of rows being dropped and change data observation later on. One solution is to count the number of NA’s in each row:

Games <- Games %>%
    mutate(na_count = rowSums(is.na(.)))

Task 2: Variable Exploration

Variable 1 - Score

The variable Score is quantitative.

countNA(Games$Score) #gets NA count
## [1] 0

Score has 0 NA’s

Games_score <-Games %>%
  summarize(
    total = sum(Score),
    mean = mean(Score),
    min = min(Score),
    Q1 = quantile(Score, .25),
    median = median(Score),
    Q3 = quantile(Score, .75),
    max = max(Score)
   
    
  )

kable(Games_score)
total mean min Q1 median Q3 max
621296 2944.531 100 1982.5 2770 3582.5 7315 
score_box <- ggplot(data = Games,
       mapping = aes(x = Score)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  labs(title = "Score Boxplot")

score_hist <- ggplot(data = Games,
       mapping = aes(x = Score)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "black", color = "grey") +
  labs(title = "Score Histogram")

score_box + score_hist
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

We can see that the eliminations variable has a positive skew, or right skew, with the mean and median being below the midpoint of the range of data. The mean is 2944.531 while the midpoint of the range is 3607.5, roughly 700 points lower. This lets us know that for most matches, this player is getting around 2700 - 3000 points, with fewer matches getting in the high range of scores.

Variable 2 - PrimaryWeapon

The variable PrimaryWeapon is categorical

levels(as.factor(Games$PrimaryWeapon)) #gets categories
## [1] "AK-47"         "FFAR 1"        "Krig 6"        "M16"          
## [5] "MG 82"         "MP5"           "Pelington 703" "QBZ-83"       
## [9] "Type 63"

PrimaryWeapon has 9 different categories, representing the most used weapon of a given match.

countNA(Games$PrimaryWeapon) #gets NA count
## [1] 0

Primary Weapon has 0 NA’s

Games_PW <- Games %>%
  group_by(PrimaryWeapon) %>%
  summarize(
     count = sum(PrimaryWeapon == PrimaryWeapon),
     percent = paste0(round(sum(PrimaryWeapon == PrimaryWeapon)/nrow(Games)*100, 2), "%")
  )

kable(Games_PW)
PrimaryWeapon count percent
AK-47 4 1.9%
FFAR 1 3 1.42%
Krig 6 21 9.95%
M16 48 22.75%
MG 82 2 0.95%
MP5 45 21.33%
Pelington 703 38 18.01%
QBZ-83 37 17.54%
Type 63 13 6.16% 
ggplot(data = Games_PW,
       mapping = aes(x = reorder(PrimaryWeapon, count), 
                     y = count)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  coord_flip() + 
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 50, by = 5)) +
  labs(x = "Primary Weapon", 
       y = "Count",
       title = "Counts of Primary Weapons")

We can see that out of the nine different primary weapons, there were four that were used the majority of the time: M16, MP5, Pellington 703, and QBZ-83, combined being the primary weapon 168 times out of the 190 total matches. There were two that were chosen a moderate amount, and three that were only chosen a combined 9 times out of 190. This distribution of primary weapons can indicate that this player had a preference for these four weapons, or that these weapons are statistically better weapons overall for the different game modes.

Variable 3 - Damage

The variable Damage is quantitative

countNA(Games$Damage) #gets NA count
## [1] 0

Damage has 0 NA’s

Games_damage <-Games %>%
  summarize(
    total = sum(Damage),
    mean = mean(Damage),
    min = min(Damage),
    Q1 = quantile(Damage, .25),
    median = median(Damage),
    Q3 = quantile(Damage, .75),
    max = max(Damage)
   
    
  )

kable(Games_damage)
total mean min Q1 median Q3 max
87600 415.1659 56 303.5 397 508 960 
damage_box <- ggplot(data = Games,
       mapping = aes(x = Damage)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  labs(title = "Damage Boxplot")

damage_hist <- ggplot(data = Games,
       mapping = aes(x = Damage)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "black", color = "grey") +
  labs(title = "Damage Histogram")

damage_box + damage_hist
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

We can see that from the histogram, the data appears to be somewhat normal, with a bell curve-like shape, with the slightest right skew. From examining the boxplot, we can confirm this seeing that there is only one outlier which is the maximum value. Removing this one value could potentially make the data even more normal and could be proven with further investigation. Overall, we can see that this player was consistent with damage around 450 with less games having either a lot more or a lot less damage done.

Task 3: Variable Relations

I have chosen the Score and PrimaryWeapon variables to compare to see if certain weapons contributed to a higher score. Score is a quantitative Variable and PrimaryWeapon is a categorical variable.

ggplot(data = Games,
       mapping = aes(x = PrimaryWeapon, y = Score)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x = "Primary Weapon",
    y = "Score",
    title = "Score Distribution by Primary Weapon"
  )

As we can see from the visualization, most of the primary weapons have a very similar score with very similar individual distributions. However, two weapons, the Type 63 and the AK-47 seem to have higher mean scores as well as higher overall scores from their distributions. This is interesting as those weapons as seen above, were used the third and fourth least out of the 9 chosen primary weapons. This could be because the player either had no intention of trying to get a high score, as these weapons were rarely used in the 190 games, or he was using other weapons for the purpose of challenges. It is also of note that the two weapons with the highest mean scores deal much more damage per shot than other weapons, which can lead to more kills as well as a faster time to kill. Eliminations add a lot of score points, which is why these weapons have the highest mean scores of all 9 primary weapons, and can be proven with further data exploration.

Task 4

# Separating Result Column
Games <- Games %>%
  separate_wider_delim(
      cols = Result, 
      delim = "-", 
      names = c("PlayerTeam", "OpposingTeam"),
      cols_remove = FALSE
    ) %>%
    mutate(across(c(PlayerTeam, OpposingTeam), as.integer))
# Getting Proportions
Games <- Games %>%
  mutate(PlayerTeamVict = ifelse(PlayerTeam > OpposingTeam, 1, 0),
         PlayerTeamLoss = ifelse(PlayerTeam < OpposingTeam, 1, 0),
         TeamTie = ifelse(PlayerTeam == OpposingTeam, 1, 0)
         )

Results <- Games %>%
  summarise(
    Won_Proportion = round(sum(PlayerTeamVict/nrow(Games)), 3),
    Lost_Proportion = round(sum(PlayerTeamLoss/nrow(Games)), 3),
    Tie_Proportion = round(sum(TeamTie/nrow(Games)), 3)
  )
kable(Results)
Won_Proportion Lost_Proportion Tie_Proportion
0.54 0.445 0.014

Task 5: Inference Question

How does Eliminations affect the odds of PlayerTeamVict when controlling for GameType and Deaths?

Model <- glm(PlayerTeamVict ~ Eliminations + Deaths + GameType, 
                   data = Games, 
                   family = "binomial")
summary(Model)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = PlayerTeamVict ~ Eliminations + Deaths + GameType, 
##     family = "binomial", data = Games)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)                 -14.42538  829.53253  -0.017 0.986126    
## Eliminations                  0.07220    0.02533   2.850 0.004375 ** 
## Deaths                       -0.11699    0.03403  -3.438 0.000587 ***
## GameTypeHC - Hardpoint       16.78351  829.53258   0.020 0.983858    
## GameTypeHC - Kill Confirmed  14.17428  829.53326   0.017 0.986367    
## GameTypeHC - TDM             15.24602  829.53219   0.018 0.985336    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 291.14  on 210  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 264.69  on 205  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 276.69
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 14
exp(coef(Model))
##                 (Intercept)                Eliminations 
##                5.434204e-07                1.074869e+00 
##                      Deaths      GameTypeHC - Hardpoint 
##                8.895927e-01                1.945301e+07 
## GameTypeHC - Kill Confirmed            GameTypeHC - TDM 
##                1.431573e+06                4.180833e+06

For every additional elimination, the odds of the player’s team winning is expected to increase by a factor of 1.075, while holding deaths and game type constant.
